common_name: Ecuador Velvet Philodendron (Verrucosum Philodendron, Velvet-Leaf Philodendron)

planting.note: Grow on a moss pole or bark support in bright indirect light with chunky, well-drained aroid mix; Philodendron verrucosum is a climbing, hemi-epiphytic species that grows best when its aerial roots have something to attach to. Maintain consistently moist but not saturated media and high humidity (60–80%+) — this species naturally grows in humid cloud forest environments. Cooler nighttime temperatures (60–65°F) can intensify the golden venation on the leaves. Propagate from stem cuttings with a node. All parts toxic if ingested.

directions: Grow in bright indirect light in chunky, well-aerated potting mix with a climbing support — Ecuador Velvet Philodendron climbs vigorously and produces progressively larger leaves as it ascends a moss pole or bark surface. Philodendron verrucosum is a hemi-epiphytic climbing aroid from the cloud forests of Ecuador, Colombia, Peru, and Costa Rica in the Araceae family, widely considered one of the most beautiful velvet-leaved philodendrons in the collector trade and globally sought by tropical plant enthusiasts. The leaves are large (up to 18–24 inches on established climbing stems), broadly cordate (heart-shaped), and covered in a dense, velvety, dark forest-green surface with a distinctive iridescent sheen and a bold pattern of yellow-gold to pale gold primary and secondary veins that stand out brilliantly against the dark velvet background. The petioles are clothed in fine, bristly hairs (the 'verrucose' quality — warty or bumpy) in shades of yellow-green to pinkish. The undersides of the leaves are deep maroon-red to burgundy — a rich color contrast visible whenever the leaves catch air movement. The leaf texture, venation pattern, and color combination make P. verrucosum one of the most photographed and desired philodendrons in modern tropical plant collecting. All parts toxic due to calcium oxalate crystals.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Philodendron verrucosum species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Climbing Philodendrons for indoor tropical collections
International Aroid Society — Philodendron verrucosum cultivation guide
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Philodendron (toxic to pets)

common_name: Giant Philodendron (Gigas Philodendron, Panama Velvet Philodendron)

planting.note: Grow on a large moss pole or tree support in bright indirect to filtered light with a chunky, free-draining aroid mix; Philodendron gigas is a vigorous climber that eventually produces leaves 3–4 feet long — allow adequate vertical and horizontal space. Juvenile leaves (the stage most commonly encountered in trade) are dark velvety olive-green with pale veins; adult leaves are enormous and more broadly ovate. Maintain consistently moist media and high humidity (60%+). Propagate from stem cuttings. All parts toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to filtered light on a large climbing support — Giant Philodendron is a vigorous climbing species that increases its leaf size dramatically as it gains height, and produces its most impressive foliage when allowed to climb freely rather than trail or hang. Philodendron gigas is a hemi-epiphytic climbing aroid endemic to Panama in the Araceae family, named 'gigas' (Greek for giant) for the ultimate size of its adult leaves — one of the most impressive of the climbing philodendrons in terms of pure foliar scale. Juvenile plants produce velvety, dark olive-green to bronze-green, broadly cordate leaves 12–18 inches long with a rich, plush texture and pale yellowish-cream to yellow venation — the velvet texture and dark color combination being similar to the highly prized P. verrucosum. As the plant climbs and matures, leaves grow progressively larger and can ultimately reach 3–4 feet in length on established plants climbing large tree trunks in its native Panamanian rainforest. The transition from the compact, velvety juvenile phase (the form most commonly grown and sold) to the enormous adult phase makes P. gigas one of the most dramatic transformation experiences in tropical aroid horticulture. An exciting, fast-growing collector's philodendron with considerable impact even in juvenile stage. All parts toxic.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Philodendron gigas species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Large Climbing Philodendrons for tropical collections
International Aroid Society — Philodendron gigas cultivation notes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Philodendron (toxic to pets)

planting.note: Grow in bright indirect light on a moss pole or bark support with chunky, well-draining aroid mix; the striking orange-yellow petioles are the most distinctive identifying feature of this species and develop best color in bright indirect light. Keep consistently moist but not waterlogged — allow the top inch to dry before re-watering. The long, lance-shaped leaves require vertical space. Tolerates humidity levels from 50–80%. Propagate from stem cuttings. A relatively fast grower among collector philodendrons. All parts toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect light in well-aerated, chunky potting mix with a climbing support — Billie's Philodendron is a hemi-epiphytic climber that grows most vigorously and produces its largest, most dramatic leaves when ascending a moss pole or other support. Philodendron billietiae is a hemi-epiphytic climbing aroid native to French Guiana, Suriname, and northern Brazil in the Araceae family, named in honor of Frieda Billiet, a Belgian botanist who collected the type specimen. The plant is distinguished in the Philodendron genus by two immediately striking characteristics: its long, bright orange to orange-yellow petioles, and its narrowly elongated, lance-shaped leaves with distinctly wavy or undulate margins — a leaf shape quite different from the broad, cordate leaves typical of many popular philodendrons. The petioles are a vivid, warm orange-yellow that contrasts brilliantly with the glossy, dark green leaf blade — the orange petioles visible from a distance and making the plant immediately recognizable among a collection of philodendrons. The leaves are large (18–36 inches on mature plants), elongated-lanceolate, with gently rippled margins and a leathery, glossy dark green texture. The mid-rib is pale yellow-green against the dark surface. A popular and relatively fast-growing collector's philodendron valued for its unique petiole color and leaf form. All parts toxic.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Philodendron billietiae species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Collector Philodendrons for tropical indoor gardens
International Aroid Society — Philodendron billietiae cultivation notes

common_name: Five Holes Plant (Standleyana Monstera, Cobra Plant, Albo Monstera)

planting.note: Grow in bright indirect light in well-draining, porous aroid mix with a climbing support; Monstera standleyana is a small, fast-growing climbing monstera that adapts well to hanging baskets or moss poles. The popular 'Albo Variegata' form has irregular cream-white to white variegation on the glossy green leaves — maintain bright indirect light to preserve variegation (dim conditions reduce variegation). Allow top inch of soil to dry between waterings. Propagate from stem cuttings. All parts toxic if ingested.

directions: Grow in bright indirect light in aerated, well-draining potting mix with support for climbing — Five Holes Plant is a small-leaved, fast-growing Monstera that produces a dense, attractive climbing display and adapts well to both support climbing and hanging basket culture. Monstera standleyana is a hemi-epiphytic climbing aroid native to Costa Rica, Panama, Colombia, and Ecuador in the Araceae family, one of the smaller-leaved Monstera species commonly grown in tropical plant collections — in contrast to the large, fenestrated (hole-bearing) foliage of M. deliciosa, M. standleyana produces smaller, oval, glossy, deep-green leaves 4–8 inches long with no holes or fenestrations even at maturity, but with a high-gloss, lacquered surface quality and compact, vigorous growth habit that has made it popular in miniature tropical gardens and as a climbing vine. The plant's name in the trade — 'Five Holes Plant' — actually refers to a different, less-common Monstera species (M. obliqua) and has been misapplied; M. standleyana itself does not develop holes. The most widely grown form is 'Albo Variegata' — a variegated cultivar with irregular cream-white to white blotching, streaking, and patterning overlaid on the glossy green leaf — one of the most sought-after Monstera variegates in the collector market. M. standleyana 'Albo' has commanded exceptionally high prices in the tropical plant hobby. All parts toxic.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Monstera standleyana species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Climbing Monstera species for tropical collections
International Aroid Society — Monstera standleyana 'Albo Variegata' cultivation
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Monstera (toxic to pets)

common_name: Dressler's Anthurium (Panama Velvet Anthurium, Bullate Anthurium)

planting.note: Grow in bright indirect to moderate indirect light in very well-draining, chunky aroid mix; Anthurium dressleri is an epiphytic species that grows naturally in the canopy of Panamanian rainforests and requires excellent drainage and aeration at the root zone — bark-based epiphyte mixes work well. Maintain consistently moist media and high humidity (70%+). Avoid cold drafts and temperatures below 60°F. This is a slower-growing, collector-grade species. Propagate from seed or stem cuttings. All parts toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect light in a chunky, bark-based, fast-draining epiphyte mix with high humidity — Dressler's Anthurium is naturally an epiphyte that perches in tree canopies and grows best in conditions that replicate that airy, moist, well-drained environment. Anthurium dressleri is an epiphytic aroid endemic to Panama in the Araceae family, named in honor of botanist Robert Louis Dressler, valued in the collector community for its extraordinarily textured leaf surface and impressive scale. The leaves are large (18–36 inches long), broadly ovate to cordate with a thick, leathery texture, and covered with a dense, velvety, dark emerald-green surface of exceptional quality. The primary feature that distinguishes A. dressleri from related velvet anthuriums is the strongly bullate (quilted or puckered) surface: the spaces between the deeply sunken primary veins are raised, forming a pattern of convex, rounded, pillow-like inter-vein panels that gives the entire leaf a distinctly three-dimensional, corrugated, quilted texture — visually similar to the quilted surface of other prized anthuriums like A. warocqueanum but with its own distinct character. The deeply impressed veins contrast with the raised surface panels to create a strongly patterned, sculptural leaf. An elite collector's anthurium for serious tropical growers with appropriate humidity and temperature conditions. All parts toxic.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Anthurium dressleri species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Collector Anthuriums for tropical indoor gardens
International Aroid Society — Anthurium dressleri cultivation notes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Anthurium (toxic to pets)
